method_core: A framework for constructing and analyzing directed interaction networks of coupled units by three complementary routes: (i) rule-based/stochastic generative models that place directed links according to simple probabilistic attachment rules; (ii) coupled nonlinear dynamical systems for the units' state variables whose equilibria are tested for local stability by linearizing and inspecting the spectrum of the Jacobian; and (iii) sequential-growth models in which the network self-assembles through iterative addition of new units followed by a selection filter that retains only viable additions, yielding large persistent structures.
query_object: directed networks of trophic (predator-prey) interactions among biological species

hypotheticals.abstract: We survey theoretical treatments of production networks, in which firms or sectors are joined by directed supplier-customer relationships. Following a summary of empirical input-output tables and firm-level transaction records, we organize the literature into three modeling traditions. The first comprises static generative models, which place directed sourcing links between agents according to simple stochastic rules calibrated to observed degree and trade-weight distributions. The second comprises dynamical models, in which the output levels of coupled producers evolve under nonlinear adjustment equations; here we concentrate on the resilience of the economy's equilibrium, characterizing when a fixed point of aggregate activity is locally stable by linearizing the coupled system and examining the spectrum of its Jacobian. Larger, more densely interlinked economies are generically harder to stabilize, reflecting a complexity-fragility tension. The third tradition builds the network endogenously: firms enter through founding or through imitation of incumbents' input recipes, and a selection filter removes entrants that cannot recover costs, so the architecture self-organizes rather than being imposed exogenously. We show that entry-and-selection processes can assemble large, persistent production structures with realistic layering and supply-cycle statistics, and that the resulting equilibria are markedly more robust than those of comparably sized randomly wired economies. We close by relating cascade-of-failure vulnerability to the generative rules that shaped the underlying topology.

Theoretical models of gene regulatory circuitry are reviewed. After summarizing genome-wide expression compendia and inferred transcription-factor interaction maps, we distinguish three classes of model. The first class are static ensemble models, which wire activating and repressing regulatory edges among genes by simple probabilistic rules and study the resulting connectivity statistics. The second class are dynamical models, in which coupled ordinary differential equations govern the abundances of transcripts and proteins across interacting loci; we emphasize the robustness of steady expression states, deriving conditions under which a fixed point of the regulatory system is asymptotically stable from the eigenvalues of the linearized dynamics. We highlight that richly interconnected circuits are not automatically stable and that specific motif structure is required to reconcile high connectivity with dynamical robustness. The third class are growth models, in which the network is enlarged over evolutionary time by duplication of existing loci followed by divergence of their regulatory inputs, with a selection step retaining only variants that preserve viable expression programs. We find that such duplication-and-divergence assembly can construct large, homeostatically stable regulatory architectures displaying the modularity and degree heterogeneity seen in real organisms, whereas equivalent randomly rewired circuits are typically dynamically fragile. We conclude by discussing how mutational network growth shapes the evolvability of developmental control.

We review models of software dependency ecosystems, where packages are nodes joined by directed 'requires' relationships. Beginning with an overview of empirical dependency graphs mined from public package registries, we group modeling efforts into three families. The first family consists of static generators, which attach dependency edges between components using simple probabilistic attachment rules tuned to reproduce observed in- and out-degree distributions and reuse frequencies. The second family consists of dynamical models, in which the load, version churn, or fault state of mutually dependent components co-evolves under coupled update equations; we focus on stability, asking when a healthy operating point of the ecosystem resists propagating breakage, and answer it by linearizing the coupled dynamics and inspecting the leading eigenvalue of the interaction matrix. We note that more densely coupled ecosystems tend toward fragility, so that heavy reuse trades functionality against susceptibility to cascading regressions. The third family consists of growth models, in which the ecosystem is built incrementally: new components are published either de novo or by forking and adapting existing ones, and an adoption filter prunes packages that attract no downstream users. We demonstrate that such publish-and-select growth assembles large, long-lived dependency structures with the layering, heavy-tailed reuse, and near-decomposability observed in real repositories, and that these are more resilient to maintainer dropout than comparably sized random graphs.

Modeling approaches for electric power transmission networks are surveyed, treating buses as nodes and directed real-power flows along transmission corridors as links. After reviewing measured grid topologies and flow records, we organize methods into three categories. The first category is static topology generators, which lay down transmission links between substations by simple stochastic construction rules reproducing empirical line-length, degree, and loading distributions. The second category is dynamical, describing the coupled electromechanical swing behavior of interconnected generators through nonlinear phase-angle equations; we concentrate on stability, establishing when a synchronized operating state is small-signal stable by linearizing about the equilibrium and evaluating the eigenvalues of the reduced system, and we show how added interconnection can either damp or, counterintuitively, undermine synchrony. The third category comprises expansion-planning and self-organized-growth models, in which the network is enlarged incrementally by commissioning new lines and generation, with a reliability-and-cost filter rejecting additions that fail security screening. We find that incremental build-out under such selection can produce large grids that remain transiently and voltage-stable at scale, exhibiting the sparse, hierarchically clustered structure of real interconnections, whereas randomly augmented grids of the same size are markedly more prone to cascading outages. We relate blackout-size statistics to the construction rules that generated the underlying topology.

hypotheticals.generic_object: directed chemical reaction networks among molecular compounds
hypotheticals.abstract: Theoretical models of chemical reaction networks are reviewed, with compounds as nodes and directed reaction transformations as links. Following a survey of curated reaction databases and mass-spectrometric inventories of complex mixtures, we distinguish three model classes. The first class are static combinatorial models, which connect reactants and products by simple rule-based schemes, such as bond-formation grammars, and characterize the resulting network topology. The second class are dynamical models, in which coupled nonlinear rate equations govern the concentrations of interacting compounds; we focus on the stability of stationary states, deriving conditions under which a steady concentration profile is asymptotically stable from the Jacobian of the mass-action kinetics, and noting that more highly connected reaction systems are not generically stable. The third class are constructive models, in which the network is grown from a small seed set by iteratively introducing new compounds via catalyzed transformations of those already present, retaining only additions that remain producible and non-depleting under the ambient flux. We show that such closure-driven growth can assemble large, self-maintaining autocatalytic organizations that persist far from equilibrium, reproducing the sparse, hierarchical connectivity of complex reaction mixtures, whereas randomly assembled networks of comparable size typically collapse to trivial steady states. We discuss implications for the emergence of self-sustaining chemistry from simple precursor pools.
